item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may be a bit late in responding to this, but I was inspired by your excellent ideas and changed the SCP from Safe to Thaumiel after introducing a new MTF.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cue the creation of MTF Mu-4 ("Slow Sippers.")

  • modified digestive tract literally able to stomach boiling water
  • targeted sympathectomy numbs pain response originating from the digestive tract
  • surgically-implanted heat sinks rapidly channels heat away from internal organs through insulated tubes, towards dissipation pads on the surface of the skin
  • active cooling system uses electricity to directly chill dissipation pads in proportional response to heat transfer increase
  • passively cooled clothing aids the operative's body tissues in conventional temperature regulation
  • fluid recycling system keeps operative hydrated, at the cost of potentially competing with anomalous water at microvilli sites in the large intestine.

I'm sure there's still an upper limit to how much water a single person can drink, but I think we can do a bit better than 100 drops.

And that's before we even consider giving the messenger an SCP-500 or two so they can survive drinking/digesting a bit longer. After all, the pills will only be consumed within the sending timeline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you know

You can put

A greater-than sign >

+ show block
– hide block

Followed by a space

between two sentences

that both start with a >

To create a line break

Without

starting

a new

quote box?


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don’t know why but I really like this article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started off pretty interesting and the general concept is pretty good, but a couple of things killed it for me. First, the escalation in that first experiment log gave me whip lash. Can't turn a tap with your hand? Time to bring out the cybernetic arm! Oops he's dead now.

The last experiment log sets up something pretty interesting, but then it just ends with a direct spelling out of exactly how the thing works, and how it works is fairly weird. These combined with some weird language choices throughout the article have nudged me into a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A dripping faucet with temporal displacement anomaly?

I'll need some time to let that sink in.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really love the idea.

And is that a motherflippin jojo reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow I really liked this one. The one thing I hate in articles is when the theoretical science (and made up science) behind an anomaly is so unbelievably outlandish or complicated that it makes the anomaly too confusing or hard to believe.

I think you have achieved the perfect balance of that here. You give just enough information to make the anomaly believable, whilst still leaving a lot of mystery as to how this object works.

Well done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's better, but still not to the point where I would upvote (the core concept doesn't really interest me, and the Foundation response doesn't quite mesh well with my personal headcanon). That said, in the interest of good faith, I will remove my downvote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your kind words.

As for the change in object class, I personally think it would be more appealing to include a change from Euclid to Safe as an additional paragraph, perhaps involving some discussion amongst Foundation management, rather than with a strikethrough. I may consider the strikethrough though if it means keeping the rest of the article streamlined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback.

I edited the piece to get rid of emphatic italicization and attempted to streamline the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to have to disagree pretty heavily with the above. There's a lot of errors throughout the piece, from excessively specific containment procedures to unnecessary italicizing that makes the piece look sort of sloppy and unprofessional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful in concise manner!

Original

Perfect amount of Randomness!

These were my thoughts after reading the article.
Congratulations author!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is very well written, and the idea represented is well-executed.

One technique that I have seen in other SCPs is the use of a strike-through to indicate the change in object class. (So the object class would be "Euclid Safe") This would be useful, since you indicate that the object class changes. Hope this helps.

EDIT: Clarity
